tweets: A lot of stalled owner deals in vacation rental management aren't lost to another company.
The owner isn't always saying no to you. They're often saying no to the hassle of switching managers.
That means the fix isn't usually more upside language about revenue, marketing, or dynamic pricing.
The fix is de-risking the move.

If the owner is worried about transition, give them a concrete path: who handles the listing migration, how existing bookings are honored, what happens if communication slips, and whether they have an exit clause.
When the switch feels safer than the status quo, deals move.
If an owner says they need to think about it, don't go straight into persuasion mode.
One of the most useful opening questions is: How long have you been thinking about making a management change?
If they've been thinking about it for months with no action, you're probably dealing with inertia, not comparison shopping.
Then follow up on one concern only. One email. One point. Too much information makes hesitation worse.
Good operators don't just make the upside sound attractive. They make the decision feel safe.
